title: Chapter 30: The War Council
content: Thecommons was packed. Every invisible who could walk — and a few who couldn't — had gathered in the underground chamber near the Glitch Garden. The mural loomed behind them: a spray-painted scream of color and defiance, the closest thing they had to a flag. Wren stood near the front, Ash at her shoulder, and watched the room fracture.

"We strike now." Ash's voice cut through the noise like a blade. He stood at the center, feet planted, jaw tight. "Before the full deployment. Hit the Hound storage facility, disable the units, buy ourselves months."
"Months." Silas leaned against a support pillar, arms crossed. The old man's face was carved with exhaustion. "And then? They build more. They always build more."
"Then we hit them again."

"You're talking about war against a corporation with government contracts." Darby's voice shook. She was near the back, arms wrapped around herself. "We can't even be seen. How do you fight something that—"
"The same way we've survived this long." Ash's hands balled into fists. "By being what they can't predict."

"They can predict us now." That from a gaunt man named Harlow, who'd been invisible longer than anyone. His voice was a rasp. "That hound found Petra in minutes. The machines learn. Every encounter feeds them data."

The room erupted. Shouting, overlapping, desperate. Some wanted to run — go deeper, find older tunnels, older hiding places. Some wanted to scatter entirely, dissolve the community, survive alone. A few wanted to fight but had no idea how.

Wren watched the chaos and felt nothing. Not numbness — clarity. The kind that comes after something breaks. Petra's scream was still in her ears, and the buzzing was still in her hands, and the fluorescent lights were still dead in the corridor where she'd lost control. Stage 4. It was coming. For all of them.
"Enough." Lira's voice. Calm, clear, cutting through the noise like a keynote speaker at a funeral.
The room turned. Lira stood on a raised section of broken flooring, her dark hair pulled back, her expression composed. She looked like she'd been waiting for this moment.

"Fighting the Hounds is a losing strategy," she said. "Running is a losing strategy. They'll find you wherever you go — deeper tunnels, scattered cells, it doesn't matter. Thermal imaging doesn't care about depth. Sonar doesn't care about walls."
"So what do you suggest?" Silas asked. His tone was wary. He'd never trusted Lira. Neither had Wren, but for different reasons.

Lira held up a hand. Between her fingers, she held a small drive — matte black, NeuroScale branded. "Before I left, I copied everything I could access. Floor plans. Security rotations. Personnel files. And this."

She projected a holographic display from the drive — a small image, washed out by the dim emergency lighting they'd rigged after Wren's burst killed the mains. It showed a device. Small. Elegant. A crystalline core encased in a titanium frame, emitting a faint pulse of light.
"The Signal Key," Lira said. "Dr. Vance's personal project. It was designed to reverse the ANX-00 glitch signal — to restore the neural pattern to its pre-treatment state."
The room went silent.
"Reverse it," someone breathed. "You mean—"

"Visible again." Lira nodded. "You become visible, the Hounds can't distinguish you from any other heat signature in a crowd. The Cleaner Protocol becomes useless. You walk out of the Underlayer and disappear into the city. Into the world."
Wren's pulse spiked. Visible. The word landed like a stone in still water, sending ripples through her chest. Visible. Seen. A face in the mirror again. A name on a register. A person.
She looked at Ash. His expression hadn't changed — still hard, still combative — but something behind it shifted. She saw it. A crack. A flicker.

"But there's a catch," Lira continued. "Always a catch. The Signal Key is in Vance's personal lab, sublevel 2 of NeuroScale's research wing. Maximum security. Biometric locks, armed response, the works."
"And you want us to break in," Ash said flatly.

"I want us to be strategic." Lira met his gaze without flinching. "The Hounds are coming in two weeks. Maybe less, after tonight. We can fight machines that learn, or we can solve the problem at the source."

"And what happens when we're visible again?" Darby's voice was raw. "We just... go back? To the world that erased us? To the jobs that fired us, the families that couldn't see us, the hospitals that turned us away?"
"You go back as evidence," Lira said. "Living proof that NeuroScale did this. You testify. You sue. You can be witnesses because you can be seen."
"Assuming Vance doesn't bury us first," Silas muttered.
The room split again, but differently now. The lines weren't between fight and flight. They were between two kinds of visibility — visible to the Hounds, or visible to the world.
Wren stepped forward. The room didn't quiet — invisibles didn't defer to hierarchy — but the arguing dampened.

"I was a patient at NeuroScale," she said. Her voice was steadier than she expected. "I took their pill because I trusted them. Because they said it would help. And they made me disappear — not just from sight, but from everything. From my life."
She paused. The mural behind her seemed to pulse in the low light.
"I don't want to stay this way. I don't want to live in tunnels and hide from machines and call this survival. I want my face back. I want my name back."
She turned to Lira. "Where exactly is the Signal Key?"
Lira's lips curved. Not a smile — a confirmation. "Dr. Vance's personal lab. Sublevel 2. I can get you in."

Ash stepped beside Wren. Close. His shoulder almost touching hers. "This is a play for visibility," he said to the room. "Not a guarantee. We get the Key, we test it, we know what we're dealing with before anyone makes a choice."
His gaze swept the crowd. Hard. Unyielding.
"But we get it. We don't wait for them to come for us."
The vote wasn't formal. Nothing in the Underlayer was. But the murmur shifted — reluctant, fearful, but shifting. Toward the Key. Toward the impossible chance of becoming real again.
Ash caught Wren's gaze. His look was unreadable, but she felt what he wasn't saying: *This isn't over. The fighting. The war. This is one battle, and you're choosing it without me.*
She chose it anyway.
After, in the corridor outside the commons, Silas stopped her. The old man's grip on her wrist was gentle but firm.
"The woman Lira," he said quietly. "She left NeuroScale, she says. Walked out with all this data, she says."
"You don't believe her."

"I believe she has an agenda. People who walk out of places like NeuroScale don't do it clean. There's always a trace." He released her wrist. "Be careful what vaults you open, Wren. Sometimes the lock is the only thing holding the door shut."
Wren nodded. She didn't tell him she already knew.
